channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Wildfires Rage in Ontario as Smoke Affects Toronto’s Air Quality
item.description: Air quality in Canada’s largest city was at times the worst in the world on Wednesday, as wildfires across Ontario sent smoke drifting into New York City and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Laura Proctor/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The CN Tower in Toronto on Wednesday as smoke from wildfires enveloped the city.

item.title: Wildfire Smoke Spreads Across the Northeast as Temperatures Spike
item.description: A heat dome is responsible for miserably high temperatures, and it’s also helping to push smoke from wildfires to the east.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Mark Walker

item.title: He Has a $25 Million Bounty on His Head but Is Also a U.S. Partner in Venezuela
item.description: A list of alleged crimes has not prevented Diosdado Cabello, and other Venezuelan officials, from working with a U.S. government that once hounded them.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Diosdado Cabello, Venezuela’s interior minister, welcomed a flight last year bringing Venezuelan migrants from Mexico.

item.title: On ‘Caribenya,’ Lido Pimienta Found a Lifeline
item.description: On Lido Pimienta’s new album, “Caribenya,” the Colombian Canadian musician sought a warmer, more complex depiction of Caribbean culture. Creating it helped her rediscover the beauty of living.
item.dc:creator: Carolina Abbott Galvão
item.media:credit: Tommy Keith for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Pimienta’s new album, images of dragonflies, rivers and the sea abound, as do depictions of fraught emotional relationships and critiques of colonialism.

item.title: For Argentina, a World Cup Semifinal Against England Isn’t Just Soccer
item.description: Wednesday’s contest for a spot in the final carries the weight of a 1986 contentious clash, a war over the Falklands and a national mythology built around Diego Maradona.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Lucía Cholakian Herrera

item.title: As Argentina Tries to Win the World Cup, the Rest of Latin America Cheers Against It
item.description: Rather than rally behind the tournament’s last Latin American team, other countries in the region are confronting Argentina’s fraught claims to its own identity.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:description: Colombia fans in yellow and Argentina fans in stripes watching Argentina playing Cape Verde earlier this month in Miami.

item.title: Chipotle Is Bringing Its Mexican-Inspired Fast Food to Monterrey, Mexico
item.description: The California-based restaurant chain is opening its first location south of the U.S.-Mexico border this week, bringing its bowls and burritos to Monterrey.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: Even though there is no shortage of burritos and tacos in Mexico, Chipotle sees a market for its customizable burrito bowls and other menu offerings south of the U.S. border.

item.title: Tornadoes in Maine? A Rare Forecast for Parts of U.S. and Canada
item.description: Storms on Tuesday could produce damaging winds, large hail and even tornadoes in areas where they are not common.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

item.title: Antonio Rattín, Whose World Cup Expulsion Led to Penalty-Card System, Dies at 89
item.description: The ejection of the Argentine soccer star during a 1966 match against England led to the introduction of yellow and red cards.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Inky Mark, Former Canada MP, Arrested After Police Find 439 Guns and Cannon in His Home
item.description: Inky Mark, a former conservative lawmaker in Manitoba, was charged with offenses including firearm trafficking.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:description: Inky Mark in Ottawa in 2005. He served as a member of Parliament from 1997 to 2010.

item.title: A Heated Rivalry Over Bull Mascots Is Dividing a Brazilian Town
item.description: Deep in the Amazon rainforest, a heated rivalry has split a town for a century. It’s got nothing to do with sports or politics.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Dado Galdieri
item.media:description: The arena in Parintins, Brazil, showing both red and blue bulls on the facade before hosting a series of performances to determine the annual winning bull.

item.title: Trump’s Threat to Impose Fees in the Strait of Hormuz Contradicts His Aides
item.description: President Trump said the United States could collect tolls or fees, despite Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying no country could do so.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ships sailing near the Strait of Hormuz off the eastern coast of the United Arab Emirates at Khor Fakkan on Monday.

item.title: A Brazilian Biopic About Jair Bolsonaro Is Threatening His Son’s Presidential Campaign
item.description: A Brazilian biopic about Jair Bolsonaro has upended his son Flávio Bolsonaro’s presidential hopes after leaks showed he negotiated money for the film with a disgraced banker.
item.dc:creator: Adam B. Ellick
item.media:description: Flávio Bolsonaro in São Paulo in June.

item.title: How Trump Failed to Secure the Strait of Hormuz in His Iran Deal
item.description: President Trump signed an agreement that Iran said gave it control of the waterway — and global energy supplies. Now, Iran’s military is violently asserting authority.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong, Michael Crowley and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump at Joint Base Andrews on Thursday. He recently said the memorandum of understanding reached with Iran was “over.”

item.title: Toronto Police Search For Active Shooter After 2 Killed at Street Festival
item.description: The shooting disrupted a weekend Latino celebration where hundreds had gathered on Saturday evening.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Police officers stand guard as emergency workers respond at the site of a deadly shooting at a salsa-themed street festival in Toronto on Saturday.

item.title: Inside the Canadian Lab Burning Down Buildings to Save Them
item.description: At the national fire safety facility in Ottawa, scientists and engineers are increasingly focused on the threat from the forest.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Ahmed Kashef, the director of the National Research Council of Canada’s fire safety lab in Ottawa, inside a furnace used to test how long walls can withstand fire.

item.title: How Marco Rubio Is Running Venezuela From Afar
item.description: The secretary of state effectively controls Venezuela’s finances, the distribution of its natural resources and its government. His grip on the country is a vivid manifestation of American power in the Trump era.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: After Being Delayed by Trump, a Canada-U.S. Bridge Is Set to Open
item.description: A deal ended the Trump administration’s blocking of a new bridge that will ease congestion at the busiest trade corridor between the United States and Canada.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Tyler Pager
item.category: Gordie Howe International Bridge (Detroit, Mich and Windsor, Ontario)
item.media:description: The Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, in February.

item.title: Ten Killed in Plane Crash on the Bahamas’ Largest Island
item.description: The crash occurred as the country celebrated its independence from Britain.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai

item.title: Mexico to File Criminal Complaints in U.S. Over Immigrant Deaths
item.description: The nation’s president vowed to go “beyond diplomatic notes” after a U.S. immigration agent fatally shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Mexican man, in Texas this week.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A candlelight vigil on Wednesday in Houston, at the spot where Lorenzo Salgado Araujo was shot and killed a day earlier.

item.title: An Aging Assassin Was Caught by ICE. Will the U.S. Deport Him?
item.description: He helped plot a car bombing in Washington that killed two people in 1976. Fifty years later, old government secrets could decide his fate.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: Armando Fernandez Larios in 2003. For years, the U.S. government regarded Mr. Fernandez as a helpful Cold War ally of sorts, even as its relationship with Chile deteriorated.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, a Youth Baseball League Counts the Dead
item.description: Children who played for Venezuela’s youth baseball league, a symbol of national pride, have been left injured, orphaned or dead.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní, Isayen Herrera and Fabiola Ferrero

item.title: Overlooked No More: Garrincha, Brazil’s Brilliant and Broken World Cup Hero
item.description: He helped lead Brazil to two championships alongside Pelé, but his on-field brilliance was eclipsed by a tragic personal life.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Sifting Through Venezuela Earthquakes’ Rubble, and the Ashes of a Revolution
item.description: The devastation of Jacqueline Zúñiga’s city in Venezuela follows the demise of her political project. The revolution is over, but the human ties that help her cope remain.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Jacqueline Zúñiga, 52, between several public housing complexes that collapsed during the twin earthquake. Ms. Zúñiga’s building was damaged and she is living in a makeshift shelter.
